intro: Scripture (from Latin scriptura, "writing"; Greek graphe) denotes the authoritative written Word of God. In the New Testament, the term consistently refers to the Hebrew writings of the Old Testament as binding divine authority — Jesus declared that "the Scripture cannot be broken" (John 10:35) and regularly appealed to it as the final word on disputed matters. Paul's statement that "all Scripture is God-breathed" (2 Tim. 3:16) remains the foundational claim for biblical inspiration and usefulness "for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness." The NT writers quote the OT as Scripture more than 250 times, and the early church quickly applied the term to the apostolic writings as well (2 Pet. 3:16). The word also appears in the singular to refer to a specific passage (Mark 12:10; John 19:37; Acts 1:16).
